title-group.article-title: Cardiorenal protective effects of glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists in chronic kidney disease: a systematic review and meta-analysis

contrib-group.aff: aDongfang Hospital, Beijing University of Chinese Medicine, Beijing, China
bXuanwu Hospital, Capital Medical University, Beijing, China
cBeijing Tongzhou District Hospital of Integrated Traditional Chinese and Western Medicine, Beijing, China
dInstitute of Basic Research in Clinical Medicine, China Academy of Chinese Medical Sciences, Beijing, China
eBeijing University of Chinese Medicine, Beijing, China

fn.p: These authors contributed equally to this work.
Supplemental data for this article can be accessed online at https://doi.org/10.1080/0886022X.2026.2620155.

author-notes.corresp: CONTACT Yu Li liyubeijing1973@163.com Beijing University of Chinese Medicine, Beijing 100029, China
Mianzhi Zhang zhangmianzhi@vip.sina.com Dongfang Hospital, Beijing University of Chinese Medicine, Beijing, 100078, China.

event.event-desc: published online in a building issue

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2026 The Author(s). Published by Informa UK Limited, trading as Taylor & Francis Group

license.license-p: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial License (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/), which permits unrestricted non-commercial use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited. The terms on which this article has been published allow the posting of the Accepted Manuscript in a repository by the author(s) or with their consent.

abstract.title: Abstract
abstract.p: Glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1 RAs) are widely used in patients with chronic kidney disease (CKD), but existing evidence regarding their efficacy and safety remains inconsistent. To evaluate the cardiorenal outcomes and adverse effects of GLP-1 RAs in this population, we conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials from PubMed, Embase, Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials, and Web of Science up to December 2024. Nine trials involving 21,717 patients, the vast majority of whom had T2DM, were included. GLP-1RAs treatment for CKD was associated with decreasing the incidence of major adverse kidney events (MAKE; RR, 0.84; 95% CI, 0.76–0.94) and major adverse cardiac and cerebrovascular events (MACE; RR, 0.84; 95% CI, 0.72–0.97), reducing all-cause mortality (RR, 0.83; 95% CI, 0.76–0.90) and albuminuria level (SMD, −1.22; 95% CI, −1.53 − 0.90). Gastrointestinal events associated with GLP-1 RA treatments including nausea (RR, 4.14; 95% CI, 2.70–6.33), vomiting (RR, 3.05; 95% CI, 1.88–4.97), diarrhea (RR, 2.65; 95% CI, 1.76–3.98), and dyspepsia (RR, 3.79; 95% CI, 1.02–14.12) have garnered significant attention. In conclusion, administration of GLP-1RAs treatment demonstrates excellent cardiorenal protective effects in CKD, primarily in patients with co-existing T2DM, though with notable gastrointestinal concerns.

institution-wrap.institution: National Natural Science Foundation of China
the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

funding-group.funding-statement: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant Nos. 82274242 and 82474223, the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities under Grant 2023-JYB-XJSJJ043, and the National Natural Science Foundation Youth Cultivation Program of Xuanwu Hospital, Capital Medical University (QNPY202516). Role of the Funder/Sponsor: The funding sources had no role in the design and conduct of the study, collection, management, analysis, and interpretation of the data; preparation, review, or approval of the manuscript; and decision to submit the manuscript for publication.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Chronic kidney disease (CKD) is a progressive condition with substantial morbidity and has emerged as one of the leading causes of mortality [1]. Albuminuria, a biomarker of kidney injury, is the major risk factor for major adverse kidney events (MAKE), and the incidence of major adverse cardiac and cerebrovascular events (MACE) [2–5]. The positive management of albuminuria that provides cardiorenal benefits remains the cornerstone in CKD treatment [6]. Nevertheless, a considerable number of patients with CKD who received current guideline-directed medical therapies for reducing albuminuria still suffer from the elevated risk of incidence of unfavorable composite cardiorenal events [7]. Hence, there is a major unmet medical need to improve the cardiorenal outcomes of patients with CKD.

Glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1RAs), as pleiotropic hormones, exert multifaceted effects: they indirectly inhibit overactivation of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS) and attenuate extracellular matrix destruction through improvements in glycemia, body weight, and blood pressure regulation, thereby conferring renal protection [8–10]. Additionally, studies demonstrated that GLP-1RAs mitigate oxidative stress and inflammation, reduce myocardial cell injury, and promote vasodilation to enhance coronary blood flow [11]. Critically, the recent clinical trial REMODEL indicates that renal benefits may also be derived from a reduction in renal sinus fat, improved hemodynamics by lowered renal vascular resistance, and stabilization of fibrosis, as indicated by a stable apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) of the renal cortex [12]. Collectively, these pathways underscore the potential mechanisms behind the cardiorenal protection observed with GLP-1RAs. However, it is important to note that the translation of some proposed mechanisms into humans requires further validation.

This mechanistic background notwithstanding, the role of GLP-1RAs in the management of CKD remains debated. Some studies report that GLP-1 RAs treatment for CKD could reduce albuminuria levels and delay the estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) loss [13–15]. Instead, some negative conclusions that GLP-1RAs were not associated with kidney benefits are worth attention [16,17] (shown in eTable 3). Meanwhile, the optimal method for applying GLP-1RAs across different stages of CKD remains poorly defined. However, recent high-quality trials provide evidence that GLP-1 RAs used in CKD with or without type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) showed promising kidney protective effects in reducing albuminuria and incidence of composite cardiorenal events [15,18].

Therefore, we conduct this study to comprehensively summarize the efficacy and safety of GLP-1RAs treatment for CKD. The results will provide the current best evidence regarding the GLP-1 RAs use profile in CKD and be helpful for clinical decision-making.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: This systematic review and meta-analysis was reported in accordance with the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses statements. The study protocol was prospectively registered with PROSPERO (registration number CRD42024550909).

sec.title: Search strategy and study screen
sec.p: We conducted a comprehensive search of the literature using appropriate prespecified search terms in PubMed, Embase, Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials, and Web of Science, from their inception dates to December 2024 without language restriction. The search strategy with search terms is presented in the Additional file: eTable 1. A secondary manual search was also performed through references to included studies to identify any additional studies not retrieved in the electronic search. After removing duplicated studies, two reviewers (L.F. and X.S.) in pairs screened the articles through abstracts and full texts successively to assess eligibility. Discrepancies were settled by discussion with a third reviewer (M.Z.).

sec.title: Inclusion and exclusion criteria
sec.p: We included RCTs that evaluated the efficacy and safety of GLP-1 RAs treatment for CKD with or without T2DM compared to usual care or placebo. Trials wherein usual care was administered equally to all groups. Studies with the cross-over design, uncontrolled clinical trials, reviews, editorials, letters, and case reports were excluded from this study.

sec.title: Data extraction
sec.p: A data extraction form was created a priori and piloted independently by two reviewers (Yu. W., and X. S.). We independently extracted data including article characteristics (authors, publication year, and country of origin), participant characteristics (age, gender, and sample size), intervention characteristics (specific GLP-1RA agents, dosage, and duration), and comparator details (usual care or placebo). The primary outcomes were the incidence of MAKE and MACE. The former included the onset of ESKD (maintenance dialysis, kidney transplant, or kidney failure without receiving kidney replacement therapy) and CKD progression (doubling of serum creatinine, onset of persistent >50% reduction in eGFR versus baseline, chronic eGFR slope, or annual rate of eGFR loss). The latter was composed of non-fatal myocardial infarction, non-fatal stroke, and cardiovascular death. Secondary outcomes included each of the individual components of the primary outcomes, all-cause mortality, and change in the proteinuria level (measured with urinary albumin to creatinine ratio [UACR]). The data regarding the incidence of adverse events were also collected to evaluate the overall safety of GLP-1 RAs treatment.

sec.title: Risk of bias assessment and grading of evidence
sec.p: Two reviewers (L.F. and Yu.W.) independently assessed the quality of each included study using the Cochrane Collaboration Risk of Bias tool. We used the Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development and Evaluation (GRADE) system for each meta-analysis to ascertain the certainty of the evidence across the trials. Two reviewers (Ya.W. and L.F.) downgraded evidence based on the risk of bias, inconsistency, indirectness, imprecision, and publication bias. Disagreements were resolved by discussion or by involving another review author (N.L.) to arbitrate.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p:
In the meta-analysis of changes in GLP-1RAs on clinical indicators of CKD, effect sizes were determined using standardized mean differences (SMD) to accommodate the diverse measurement methods used across studies, including the eGFR and UACR [19]. Approximate values (to the nearest decimal place) were derived from the graphs when exact mean and standard deviation (SD) values were not reported in the included articles. SEs were converted to SD by multiplying them by the square root of the sample size. Pooled means were calculated by adding all the means, multiplying by their sample size, and then dividing by the sum of all sample sizes. Pooled SD was calculated with the following formula:
SDpooled=((SD12[n1−1])+(SD22[n2−1])+…+(SDk2[nk−1])/(n1+n2+…+nk−k)), where n indicates sample size and k indicates the number of samples [20].

Statistical analyses were performed by using STATA 18.0 and Revman 5.4 software. We calculated relative risk (RR) from the number of events and participants. The effect estimates of dichotomous data were measured by RR with a 95% CI. Statistical heterogeneity amongst the included trials will be evaluated using the I2 test, and a meta-analysis will be conducted if there proves to be no significant clinical (relating to the participants, interventions, controls, and outcomes) and statistical heterogeneity (I2 values are less than 50%). We will use a fixed-effect model to synthesize the data if the I2 value is less than 50%. We will find the sources of the heterogeneity when I2 value is higher than 50%. If the statistical heterogeneity is explained successfully by sensitive analysis or subgroup analysis, we will also use the fixed-effect model to synthesize the data, otherwise, a random-effects model will be applied. Subgroup analyses were stratified by CKD stages and complications of T2DM. A 2-tailed p < 0.05 was considered statistically significant. The potential publication bias was assessed by visual inspection of the symmetry of funnel plots and validated by the Egger test if at least 10 trials for each outcome were available [21,22].

sec.title: Characteristics of the included studies
sec.p: From the initial 517 records retrieved, 183 duplicates were removed, 45 were marked as letters, comments, and errata, and 45 were removed as protocol. Subsequently, 239 records were screened, and 50 records of systematic reviews and network analyses were excluded. Then, 189 reports accessed for eligibility were screened by title and abstract, resulting in the exclusion of 181 records that did not meet the research type. Additionally, 1 study was manually included through retrieval. A total of 9 reports representing 9 independent studies were included in the review. The study selection process is shown in Figure 1.

caption.p: PRISMA flow diagram showing selection of randomized controlled trials of GLP-1RAs (semaglutide, liraglutide, dulaglutide, exenatide, cotadutide) in patients with CKD.

sec.p: Across the 9 studies included [15,18,23–29], a total of 21,717 patients with CKD were enrolled. The main characteristics of the included studies were summarized in Table 1. Five types of GLP-1RAs are involved among the 9 studies, namely liraglutide, dulaglutide, exenatide, cotadutide, and semaglutide. These studies were published from 2016 to 2024. Eight studies [18,23–29] with a total of 21,616 participants were CKD and T2DM. The eGFR ranged from 20 to over 90 mL/min/1.73m2. The participants in 4 studies [24–26,29] were in stage G3-G4 (15 < eGFR < 60 mL/min/1.73 m2), and the participants in 2 studies [15,25] were overweight (BMI > 25 kg/m2). As for study design, 7 studies [15,18,23–26,28] used placebo as the control. The outcomes from this comparison are synthesized in Table 2, which provides a summary of findings for GLP-1RAs versus placebo in the CKD population.

fn.p: GLP-1RAs: glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists; T2DM: type 2 diabetes mellitus; BMI: body mass index; CV: cardiovascular; eGFR: estimated glomerular filtration rate; HbA1c: hemoglobin A1c; UACR: urine albumin-creatinine ratio; MMTT: mixed meal tolerance test; AUC: area under the curve.

caption.p: Summary of findings table comparing effects of GLP-1 receptor agonists versus placebo or standard care in people with chronic kidney disease.

tr.th: GLP-1 receptor agonists compared to placebo or standard care for people with chronic kidney disease
Patients or population: people with chronic kidney diseaseSetting: Multicenter, international randomized controlled trials.Intervention: GLP-1 receptor agonistsComparison: placebo or standard care
Risk with placebo or standard care

tr.td: Compared to placebo, GLP-1 receptor agonists reduce the risk of MAKE in people with CKD and T2D.
Compared to placebo, GLP-1 receptor agonists reduce the risk of MACE in people with CKD and T2D.
Compared to placebo, GLP-1 receptor agonists probably have little to no effect on the risk of end-stage renal disease in people with CKD and T2D.
Onset of persistent 50% reduction in eGFR versus baseline
Compared to placebo, GLP-1 receptor agonists reduce the risk of a sustained ≥50% reduction in eGFR in people with CKD and T2D.
Compared to placebo, GLP-1 receptor agonists reduce the risk of cardiovascular death in people with CKD and T2D.
Compared to placebo, GLP-1 receptor agonists probably have little to no effect on the risk of non-fatal myocardial infarction in people with CKD and T2D.
Compared to placebo, GLP-1 receptor agonists may have little to no effect on the risk of non-fatal stroke in people with CKD and T2D.
Compared to placebo or standard care, GLP-1 receptor agonists reduce all-cause mortality in people with CKD and type 2 diabetes.
Compared to placebo or standard care, GLP-1 receptor agonists probably reduce the UACR in people with CKD.
Compared to placebo or standard care, GLP-1 receptor agonists may have little to no effect on the eGFR in people with CKD.
Compared to placebo or standard care, GLP-1 receptor agonists reduce the risk of serious adverse events (SAEs) in people with CKD.
Compared to placebo or standard care, GLP-1 receptor agonists reduce the risk of hypoglycemia in people with CKD.
Compared to placebo or standard care, GLP-1 receptor agonists increase the risk of nausea in people with CKD.
Compared to placebo or standard care, GLP-1 receptor agonists increase the risk of vomiting in people with CKD and T2D.
Compared to placebo or standard care, GLP-1 receptor agonists increase the risk of diarrhea in people with CKD.
Compared to placebo or standard care, GLP-1 receptor agonists probably increase the risk of constipation in people with CKD.
Compared to placebo, GLP-1 receptor agonists probably increase the risk of dyspepsia in people with CKD.

fn.p: The risk in the intervention group (and its 95% confidence interval) is based on the assumed risk in the comparison group and the relative effect of the intervention (and its 95% Cl).

Cl: confidence interval; RR: risk ratio; SMD: standardized mean difference; MAKE: composite kidney outcomes of major adverse kidney events; MACE: major adverse cardiac and cerebrovascular events; SAEs: serious adverse events.
GRADE Working Group grades of evidence.
High certainty: we are very confident that the true effect lies close to that of the estimated of the effect.

Moderate certainty: we are moderately confident in the effect estimate: the true effect is likely to be close to the estimate of the effect, but there is a possibility that it is substantially different.
Low certainty: our confidence in the effect estimate is limited; the true effect may he substantially different from the estimate of the effect.

sec.title: Risk of bias
sec.p: We found that all the studies included were judged to have a low risk of bias. This quality risk assessment indicated all studies to be of good quality. The detailed bias assessment results of each included study can be found in the Additional file: eFig. 1–2.

sec.title: Major adverse kidney events
sec.p: We pooled available data from 2 trials [18,28] involving 16,458 patients. A total of 997 patients with CKD experienced MAKE, and the risk of composite kidney outcome was significantly lower in the GLP-1RAs group compared with controls (RR, 0.84; 95% CI, 0.76 to 0.94, I2 = 6%, p = 0.001) (Figure 2).

caption.p: Forest plot of the effect of GLP-1RAs on MAKE, including CKD progression, eGFR decline, and end-stage kidney disease, in randomized controlled trials of CKD patients.

sec.title: Major adverse cardiac and cerebrovascular events
sec.p: We utilized data from 3 trials [18,24,28] with 20,382 patients. GLP-1 RAs treatment significantly reduced the incidence of MACE compared with controls (RR, 0.84; 95% CI, 0.72 to 0.97, I2 = 68%, p = 0.02) (shown in Figure 3). After excluding EXSCEL trials for sensitivity analysis (shown in eFig. 3), the results for MACE remained consistent (RR, 0.78; 95% CI, 0.69 to 0.88, I2 = 26%, p < 0.01) (shown in eFig. 4).

caption.p: Forest plot of GLP-1RAs (semaglutide, liraglutide, exenatide) on MACE in CKD populations with or without type 2 diabetes.

sec.title: Onset of end-stage renal disease
sec.p: There were no observed benefits for GLP-1RAs in patients with CKD about the incidence of end-stage renal disease based on 2 studies [18,28] involving 16,548 patients (RR, 0.88; 95% CI, 0.67 to 1.15, I2 = 0%, p = 0.34) (shown in eFig. 5).

sec.title: Onset of persistent >50% reduction in eGFR versus baseline
sec.p: Among 2 trials [18,28] including 16,458 patients, GLP-1RAs treatment exhibited a significantly lower risk of an eGFR decline greater than 50% compared to those in the placebo group (RR, 0.84; 95% CI, 0.74 to 0.96, I2 = 23%, p < 0.01) (shown in eFig. 6).

sec.title: Cardiovascular death
sec.p: A total of 3 studies [18,24,28] involving 20,382 participants showed that patients treated with GLP-1RAs exhibited a significantly lower risk of cardiovascular death compared to those in the placebo group (RR, 0.81; 95% CI, 0.72 to 0.90, I2 = 43%, p < 0.01) (shown in eFig. 7).

sec.title: Non-fatal myocardial infarction
sec.p: The meta-analysis of 3 studies [18,24,28] (n = 20,382) found no significant association between the risk of non-fatal myocardial infarction and GLP-1RAs treatment (RR, 0.93; 95% CI, 0.83 to 1.03, I2 = 37%, p = 0.18) (shown in eFig. 8).

sec.title: Non-fatal stroke
sec.p: Across 3 studies [18,24,28] including 20,382 patients, there were no observed benefits of the incidence of non-fatal stroke in GLP-1RAs treatment using a random-effect model (RR, 0.84; 95% CI, 0.57 to 1.24, I2 = 77%, p = 0.37) (shown in eFig. 9). The sensitivity analysis was represented in eFig. 10.

sec.title: All-cause mortality
sec.p: In 5 trials [18,23,24,28,29] with available data specifically for 21,205 patients, a total of 1,909 patients experienced all-cause mortality events, and the risk was significantly lower in the GLP-1RAs group compared with the placebo group (RR, 0.83; 95% CI, 0.76 to 0.90, I2 = 0%, p < 0.01) (shown in Figure 4).

caption.p: Forest plot showing impact of GLP-1RAs on all-cause mortality in CKD, pooled across randomized controlled trials.

sec.title: Urinary albumin-to-creatinine ratio
sec.p: 3 articles [15,23,27] reported the relative change in UACR before and after treatment. There was statistical heterogeneity among the included studies (I2 = 65%). A random-effects model was used to combine the effect sizes. The results showed that compared with the control group, GLP-1RAs could significantly decrease the UACR level after the intervention (SMD, −1.02; 95% CI, −1.41 to −0.63, p < 0.01) (shown in eFig. 11). Leave-one-out sensitivity analysis showed that excluding a single study did not significantly alter the overall trend in the effect size of change in UACR between the GLP-1RAs and placebo groups (SMD, −1.22; 95% CI, −1.53 to −0.90, I2 = 0%, p < 0.01) (shown in eFig. 12–13). Subgroup analysis stratified by CKD stage indicated heterogeneous responses in UACR. Specifically, the subgroup of CKD stages 1–2 (comprising 2 studies [15,27], n = 183) demonstrated significantly greater reductions in UACR with GLP-1RAs treatment (SMD, −1.22; 95% CI, −1.53 to −0.90, p < 0.01) compared to the subgroup of CKD stages 3–5 (1 study [23], n = 248) (SMD: −0.70, 95% CI: −1.01 to −0.38, p < 0.01) (shown in eFig. 14). The analysis also demonstrated that GLP-1RAs significantly reduced UACR levels across T2DM subgroups, without significant between-subgroup difference [(SMD: −1.00, 95% CI: −1.63 to −0.37, p < 0.01) vs. (SMD: −1.10, 95% CI: −1.54 to −0.66, p < 0.01); interaction, p = 0.80] (shown in eFig. 15).

sec.title: Estimated glomerular filtration rate
sec.p: 4 studies [15,25,27,29] including 797 patients showed there was no significant improvement in eGFR levels observed among CKD patients treated with GLP-1 RAs (SMD, 0.57; 95% CI, −0.18 to 1.32, I2 = 93%, p = 0.14) (shown in eFig. 16). However, subgroup analysis revealed a statistically significant improvement in eGFR levels, specifically in CKD stage 3–5 patients receiving GLP-1 RAs (SMD, 0.16; 95% CI, 0.00 to 0.33, I2 = 0%, p = 0.05) (shown in eFig. 17).

sec.title: Serious adverse event
sec.p: Eight studies [15,18,23–27,29] covering 7025 participants showed GLP-1RAs treatment was consistent with a lower incidence of serious adverse events occurring in patients with CKD (RR: 0.92; 95% CI, 0.88 to 0.97, I2 = 0%, p < 0.01) (shown in eFig. 18).

sec.title: Hypoglycemic
sec.p: Seven studies [15,18,23–25,27,29] showed GLP-1RAs reduce the incidence of hypoglycemic events (RR: 0.68; 95% CI, 0.53 to 0.87, I2 = 7%, p < 0.01) (shown in eFig. 19).

sec.title: Gastrointestinal events
sec.p: In the included studies, gastrointestinal adverse events occurred at a higher frequency in the GLP-1RAs group versus the placebo group. Higher risks of nausea (RR, 4.14; 95% CI: 2.70 to 6.33, I2 = 0%, p < 0.01) (shown in eFig. 20) [15,23,25,26,29], vomiting (RR, 3.05; 95% CI: 1.88 to 4.97, I2 = 0%, p < 0.01) (shown in eFig. 21) [18,23,25,26,29], diarrhea (RR, 2.65; 95% CI, 1.76 to 3.98, I2 = 0%, p < 0.01) (shown in eFig. 22) [15,18,23,25,26,29], constipation (RR, 2.02; 95% CI, 1. 31 to 3.61, I2 = 0%, p < 0.05) (shown in eFig. 23) [15,23,26,29] and dyspepsia (RR, 3.79; 95% CI, 1.02 to 14.12, I2 = 0%, p = 0.05) (shown in eFig. 24) [15,23,25] were observed with GLP-1RAs compared with placebo.

sec.title: GRADE assessment
sec.p: All outcome measurements were rated using GRADE, with evidence quality rated as low to high (shown in eTable 2). Specifically, the evidence was rated as high for MACE, MAKE, all-cause mortality, cardiovascular death, non-fatal myocardial infarction, onset of end-stage renal disease, persistent 50% decline in eGFR from baseline, diarrhea, hypoglycemia, vomiting, and SAEs, while as moderate for change in UACR, dyspepsia, nausea, as well as constipation, and as low for changes in eGFR and non-fatal stroke. Inconsistency and imprecision were the primary reasons for downgrading the evidence.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Our systematic review and meta-analysis found that GLP-1 RAs treatment for CKD, especially those with T2DM, significantly reduces the incidence of MAKE and MACE, all-cause mortality, and albuminuria level, yet was associated with an elevated risk of gastrointestinal adverse events. To our knowledge, our study is the first systematic review that comprehensively summarizes the efficacy and safety profile of GLP-1 RAs treatment in CKD with or without T2DM, uniquely incorporating recent pivotal evidence such as the Apperloo et al. [15] trial, which exclusively enrolled non-diabetic CKD patients, offering unique insights into the GLP-1RA effects in a non-diabetic CKD population. This inclusion significantly enhances the understanding of the clinical efficacy of GLP-1RAs in CKD populations across the full glycemic spectrum.

Despite the widespread implementation of guideline-directed medical therapy for CKD, the residual risks of MAKE remain a challenge for CKD management, especially in populations with uncontrolled proteinuria [30]. GLP-1 RAs agents exert renal protective effects through decreases in inflammation, oxidative stress, and fibrosis [31,32]. Nevertheless, GLP-1 RAs agents have not been generally recommended in CKD treatment strategy [33]. After integrating the evidence from currently available trials, we found that GLP-1 RAs treatment for CKD demonstrated excellent renal protective effects in reducing the incidence of MAKE. We advise interpreting and applying the findings with power as the certainty of evidence was rated as high.

Cardiac and cerebrovascular events constitute the predominant cause of mortality in patients with CKD [34]. GLP-1 RAs agents confer cardiovascular protection by regulating myocardial energy metabolism and enhancing both systolic and diastolic cardiac function [35]. Our findings, supported by high-certainty evidence of consistent cardiovascular benefits across trials with minimal risk of bias, strongly reinforce the rationale for integrating GLP-1RAs into evidence-based clinical guidelines for CKD management. Consequently, our findings provide robust validation for the significant cardiorenal benefits of GLP-1RAs in patients with CKD and concomitant T2DM.

CKD has emerged as a leading cause of mortality and affects an expanding population worldwide [1,36]. Unexpectedly, our meta-analysis provides high-certainty evidence that GLP-1RAs agents significantly reduce all-cause mortality in patients with CKD. Studies attribute these protective effects to multiple mechanisms, including cardiovascular and renal protection, metabolic improvements via glucose regulation and weight loss, and potential reduction in risks of cognitive impairment [37–39]. Given these findings, clinicians may consider prescribing GLP-1RAs for patients with CKD to improve long-term prognosis.

Albuminuria is widely accepted as the most widely recognized and strongest predictor of unfavorable renal outcomes in CKD. Lower albuminuria levels predict a reduced risk of adverse renal outcomes [40,41], and aggressive interventions targeting albuminuria show the potential to delay ESKD progression [42]. UACR serves as a tool for screening and evaluating albuminuria. After pooling all currently available included studies, our meta-analysis confirmed the UACR-improving effects of GLP-1RAs in patients with CKD with a moderate certainty of evidence. This finding remained robust upon sensitivity analysis validation. Importantly, our study provides the first evidence that GLP-1RAs treatment maintains a protective effect on UACR in CKD patients, including those with and without T2DM. Most notably, we provide the first meta-analytic evidence of a stage-specific effect: the UACR-lowering benefit of GLP-1RAs was significantly stronger in the early-stage (CKD 1–2) population compared to those in later stages (CKD 3–5). This novel finding of a graded benefit, robust across sensitivity analyses, positions GLP-1RAs as a particularly promising therapy for early CKD management and offers critical insights for guiding treatment strategies and updating clinical guidelines.

Notably, our meta-analysis revealed a substantially elevated risk of gastrointestinal adverse events with GLP-1RAs use, supported by evidence graded from moderate to high certainty. The increasing risk of vomiting and diarrhea was supported by high-certainty evidence, while nausea, constipation, and dyspepsia were established with moderate-certainty evidence due to small sample sizes and potential sponsor-related bias. This detailed quantification aligns with the drugs’ known mechanisms of delayed gastric emptying [43]. In contrast to the increased GI risks, high-grade evidence demonstrated a slight reduction of serious adverse events with GLP-1Ras (RR: 0.92 [95% CI 0.88–0.97]). This finding corroborates and updates prior meta-analyses (OR: 0.78, 95% CI: 0.66 to 0.92, p = 0.003) [13]. Additionally, several studies reported the risk of other gastrointestinal events like intestinal obstruction and pancreatitis associated with GLP-1RAs use [44,45], which aligned with previous research. This moderate-to-high certainty of evidence emphatically highlights the need to weigh these risks against the significant cardiorenal benefits when prescribing GLP-1RA, particularly for patients with preexisting or at high risk of gastrointestinal disorders.

Previous studies suggested that GLP-1RAs provide cardiovascular and renal protection in patients with T2DM. However, these earlier trials were primarily designed to assess cardiovascular outcomes (CVOTs) and enrolled high-risk cardiovascular populations. Earlier corresponding meta-analyses often included patients based solely on reduced baseline eGFR, resulting in diagnostic ambiguity and a lack of studies specifically focused on CKD populations. The FLOW trial successfully addressed this gap by evaluating GLP-1RAs in patients with CKD and T2DM, and both the KDIGO 2024 and ADA 2025 guidelines have newly incorporated recommendations for GLP-1RAs in patients with CKD and T2DM who have not achieved glycemic targets [33,46,47]. By integrating the FLOW trial and other relevant studies, our results further reinforce and support this clinical practice (Figure 5). Our work explicitly focuses on the established CKD population and evaluates a broad spectrum of clinical outcomes to comprehensively investigate the efficacy of GLP-1RAs in patients with CKD. Our meta-analysis provides updated evidence supporting the use of GLP-1RAs in CKD, particularly in those with comorbid T2DM. Furthermore, for non-diabetic CKD patients, we extend the promising evidence supporting their renalprotective potential, and we first report that early administration of GLP-1RAs leads to a reduction in proteinuria in CKD with or without T2DM.

caption.p: When to consider GLP-1RAs in CKD.

sec.p: We acknowledge the potential limitations of meta-analysis, mainly led by bias in included RCTs in terms of study design. We focus on the occurrence of clinical events and changes in objective laboratory indicators to avoid measurement bias for the reason that incomplete blinding designs may lead to an overestimation of the therapeutic effects. Additionally, the imprecision in changes in eGFR within the GRADE evidence profile was rated as serious due to the inconsistency and imprecision of the included studies. Consequently, we conducted sensitivity analyses and subgroup analyses to explore potential sources of heterogeneity and enhance the robustness of our findings. Our study provides the first systematic evaluation of GLP-1 RAs in non-T2DM CKD populations, demonstrating their cardiorenal protective effects in a broader CKD patient cohort. It is important to note that, although the overall quality of evidence was rated as high according to the GRADE framework, this meta-analysis has limitations. Despite enrolling a broadly defined CKD population, the limited number of non-T2DM participants constrains the generalizability of GLP-1 RA efficacy in this subgroup. Consequently, the current evidence remains insufficient to support updates to international recommendations. Further validation in non-T2DM CKD cohorts is essential to more comprehensively evaluate the efficacy and safety of GLP-1 RAs in this specific population. Additionally, due to safety constraints in clinical trial recruitment, our findings are most robust for patients with moderate CKD, while their applicability to those with late-stage disease remains limited. Therefore, a more comprehensive CKD population—including non-diabetic individuals and all disease stages—large, well-designed RCTs focusing explicitly on these cohorts are urgently needed to validate the cardiorenal protective effects of GLP-1 RAs across a more comprehensive CKD population. We believe that the growing application of GLP-1RAs in comprehensive CKD will likely provide further definitive evidence.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: Our systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrated that GLP-1RAs treatment conferred excellent cardiorenal protective effects in reducing the incidence of MAKE and MACE, and all-cause mortality in patients with CKD, particularly those complicated by T2DM. Reduction of proteinuria was associated with GLP-1RAs treatment regardless of the presence of T2DM. Notably, GLP-1RAs agents are associated with an elevated risk of gastrointestinal adverse events, which emphasizes the need to balance the risks and benefits when making clinical decisions.

ack.title: Acknowledgement
ack.p: The authors acknowledge contributions from all the included studies.

sec.title: Disclosure statement
sec.p: The authors declare that they have no competing interests.

sec.title: Ethics statement
sec.p: A Statement of Ethics is not applicable because this study is based exclusively on published literature.

sec.title: Data deposition
sec.p: Not applicable.

sec.title: Data availability statement
sec.p: Data sharing is not applicable to this article as no new data were created or analyzed in this study.
